## [06:05 - 09:08]

millions of dollars of sales and helped tens of thousands, probably hundreds of thousands, maybe millions of clients. You do your first launch and then you get better. And I'll tell you, so this is just a really rough diagram of John. I've gotten to know John very well. Rough diagram of his business, different from a diagram of my business and every business is going to diagram out a little bit differently. But this is the kind of path. You get that first launch done, you get better. You add in a back end, which is the sales you make to the people who've already bought from you. You add in continuity is a great thing, a membership site. So you're getting paid every single month and you take care of your clients. Like one of my big themes is you make that first sale not really for the money. I mean, you absolutely want the money. You need the money. You're running a business. You've got costs you have to have the money, but you really are making that sale to generate a raving fan. You take care of your clients and they're going to take care of you going forward. You want to look around your market. You want to look at what other people are doing in your market. You want to look at the kind of offers they're making. You want to look at your so-called competitors and see how are they marketing? What are they marketing? What's their offer? And you probably want to buy their products and take a full look at what they're doing. You want to look laterally at other businesses in other markets. Maybe you're in the herb market. Maybe you look at the guitar market. Maybe you look at my market. You just take a look at what other people are doing and pull laterally from those other ideas. And you really want to respect the ecosystem. That's a phrase you'll hear a lot from me, especially if you come out to one of my live events. It's amazing how being nice can be a competitive advantage. And then you want to stay plugged in. Like, I mean, stay plugged into the PLF community. the idea for his membership site that's now doing $40,000 a month. That was one idea from events. So go to events. I can't stress how important that is. Another thing you have to be really careful of is that as your business grows, the deal flow will grow. So as John gets here and he starts to build up this membership, like all of a sudden everyone in their brother comes to him and they want to make a deal. They want to do some kind of a deal. They want them to launch their product. They want to promote their product. All kinds of different. You want to be on your TV show, whatever. There's all kinds of deals that will come for you. That's what we call a deal flow. And as your business grows and your positioning grows, your deal flow grows. Absolutely. Right now, like I said, John does a couple courses a year of where he's taking other people's, there, the other people are actually creating content, creating the course with him, for him as part of his business. It's a great business. But right now, he could probably be doing 20 or 30 of these deals. Because there are so many people that see the results that he's getting. And so the deal flow is immense. So you have to be really careful. One of the most important concepts in business is this concept of opportunity cost. If you take one opportunity, if you take one deal, you're saying no to a whole bunch of other deals. So as your business grows, your judgment has to grow and you have to be able to judge which deal to take. One of my favorite scenes is that every yes that you give to a deal, every yes must be defended by a thousand no's. So you have to say no to a thousand different things and pick out of that thousand that one yes. And your ability to leverage your business up the way John has will depend on your ability to

## [12:11 - 15:12]

pick the right flow and to pick the right deal out of that huge deal flow that will come to you. When you're starting out, when you're over here, there's no deal flow. No one wants to do a deal with you. No one cares about you when you're selling 12 board games. But as you move through here and you start to build your positioning, you start to build your list, you start to build the size of your business, you start to build your assets, you start to build your capabilities, all of a sudden that deal flow comes to you. And at first it's intoxicating, it's exciting, but very quickly you have to be very discerning in which deal to take. And as you move forward and you start to make more money, one of the things you have to do is you can't be too cool for school. You have to stay in that learning mode. You have to take that money and turn it into time and take that time and turn it into study and evolution. That's really what this video is about. It's about learning, getting better and evolution. And what I mean by taking that money and turning it into time, at some point, at some point you're going to be able to start hiring people to do things, to do the menial things, or just do the things you're not great at. And that is how you buy time. And it's important to take that time and recharge and grow your mental game. And I'll be talking about that in another lesson. And part of that is taking time and carving it out for study. This is a deal. When you signed up for this business, or you signed up for any business, you signed up for constant evolution, constant learning, and constant study. Another thing you have to do is you have to think long term. You can't think about how much money am I going to make with this launch. It's okay to think about that, but if you've too much focus on this launch or this month, it takes away from your judgment in terms of one year. What is this decision going to make a year from now? Three years from now, five years from now? When I'm looking at potential partners, especially joint venture partners, I'm thinking like, do I want to work with this person for the next five years? Am I looking, is this going to be a good five year relationship? I'm not looking at what will happen if they promote for me or I promote for them. I'm saying, what is this relationship going to look like five years from now? Who am I going to be and who are they going to be? And are we still going to be aligned? So taking a long view, thinking long term is absolutely huge and having this type of growth. And then finally or nearly finally, a lot of what I mentioned like at some point, it's okay. You got to know the rules and you got to follow the rules. And once you know those rules, it's okay to break the rules occasionally. If you know that you're breaking them and you're doing it for a strategic reason. So for instance, there could be things like I've seen at times where people have gone all out to win maybe a JV competition, a joint venture competition, a joint

venture launch, because they wanted the positioning of having one that they wanted to build that relationship with that joint venture partner.
